Cause or Signature? The “Village” and Parental Depression in FFCWS

Latent trajectory clustering and a four-rung causal ladder testing whether social support moves parental depression

SSPP Research

2026-06-26

Scope & framing. This notebook covers the FFCWS analysis end to end: data construction, latent trajectory clustering, and within-person modeling of social support (“the Village”) and parental depression.

Key framing. This is a social-support → parental-depression study covering mothers and fathers; cohort = births 1998–2000. The outcome is binary CIDI-SF major-depression caseness — the only depression measure repeated at all four waves for both parents.

1 Configuration

WAVES <- c(2, 3, 4, 5)                                  # 2=Year1, 3=Year3, 4=Year5, 5=Year9
WAVE_LABEL <- c("2"="Year 1","3"="Year 3","4"="Year 5","5"="Year 9")
GAD_WAVES <- c(2, 3)                                    # GAD caseness fielded Years 1 & 3 only

# Feature engineering lives ONCE in the canonical pipeline (scripts/pipeline/, defined in
# sources.py and validated by `pytest scripts/pipeline/tests`). This document is a pure
# CONSUMER of the resulting marts — no inline re-derivation from the raw .dta. The 6-item
# perceived-support battery, the enacted (received-from-kin) and grandparent (co-residence)
# facets, and the graded family-history construct all originate there.
MARTS <- list(caseness = file.path(PROC, "ffcws_parental_mh_long.csv"),  # CIDI caseness panel
              vuln     = file.path(PROC, "preexisting_vuln.csv"),        # family-history + CES-D
              village  = file.path(PROC, "village_wave.csv"),            # wave-level village facets
              panel    = file.path(PROC, "village_panel_tv.csv"))        # lagged-transition panel

2 Preprocessing — load the canonical feature marts

This document loads the tested marts produced by scripts/pipeline/ (run python3 -m scripts.pipeline.build): the CIDI caseness panel, the family-history / CES-D vulnerability table, the wave-level Village facets, and the lagged-transition panel. Every definition — the 6-item perceived-support battery, the enacted (received-from-kin) and grandparent (co-residence) facets, and the graded multi-symptom family history — is shared with the predictive pipeline.

# (a) CIDI caseness panel + covariates
long <- read.csv(MARTS$caseness) |>
  filter(wave %in% WAVES) |>
  transmute(idnum = as.integer(idnum), parent, wave,
            md_case_lib, md_case_con, gad_case, poverty_cat, par_edu, par_age) |>
  filter(!(is.na(md_case_lib) & is.na(md_case_con)))
write.csv(long, file.path(PROC, "rmd_caseness_long.csv"), row.names = FALSE)

# (b) family-history vulnerability + father baseline CES-D (graded columns also present)
vuln <- read.csv(MARTS$vuln)

# (c) wave-level Village facets (perceived / enacted / grandparent / realized)
support_long <- read.csv(MARTS$village) |> mutate(idnum = as.integer(idnum))

cat(sprintf("marts loaded | caseness rows %d | fh prevalence mother %.3f / father %.3f | father CES-D mean %.1f\n",
            nrow(long), mean(vuln$fh_mother, na.rm=TRUE), mean(vuln$fh_father, na.rm=TRUE),
            mean(vuln$cesd_father_baseline, na.rm=TRUE)))
## marts loaded | caseness rows 28674 | fh prevalence mother 0.335 / father 0.296 | father CES-D mean 13.2

What the marts encode. fh substitutes for the pre-partum baseline FFCWS never collected — family history of depression (bio-parent ever depressed 2wk+), asked at Year 3 and filled from Year 5 for coverage (zero Y3/Y5 overlap, so a union not a re-measure). The mart also carries graded fh_*_burden/severity/suicide; the models below use the binary fh_mother/fh_father. v_instrumental is the 6-item battery (loan $200/$1000, place to live, emergency childcare, co-sign $1000/$5000).

Note on fh measurement (recall bias). A single retrospective family-history report is colored by the respondent’s mood at interview (mood-congruent recall — depressed respondents over-report). FFCWS asked the Year-5 items only of those missed at Year 3 (zero overlap), so a test–retest is impossible and the union adds coverage only. The recall-bias caveat stands as a limitation; it cannot be differenced out.

Deliberate exclusion: the childcare/partner-care facet is not pooled — its response scale differs across waves and it carries an informative “no father to count on” code. The enacted facet (v_enacted, borrowed-from-kin) is kept as a SEPARATE feature (expected reverse-signed via support mobilization), never folded into the perceived-support index.

2.1 Discovery / holdout split (family level)

fams <- sort(unique(long$idnum))
disc_ids <- sample(fams, size = floor(0.6 * length(fams)))
long$split <- ifelse(long$idnum %in% disc_ids, "discovery", "holdout")

3 Exploratory summary

long |> filter(!is.na(md_case_lib)) |>
  group_by(parent, wave) |>
  summarise(n = n(), dep_rate = mean(md_case_lib), .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(child_age = WAVE_LABEL[as.character(wave)]) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3, caption = "CIDI major-depression caseness prevalence by wave and parent")
CIDI major-depression caseness prevalence by wave and parent
parent wave n dep_rate child_age
father 2 3376 0.104 Year 1
father 3 3291 0.143 Year 3
father 4 3146 0.116 Year 5
father 5 2647 0.144 Year 9
mother 2 4362 0.155 Year 1
mother 3 4221 0.206 Year 3
mother 4 4129 0.170 Year 5
mother 5 3502 0.175 Year 9 
long |> filter(!is.na(md_case_lib)) |>
  group_by(parent, wave) |> summarise(dep = mean(md_case_lib), .groups="drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(wave, dep, color = parent)) + geom_line(linewidth = 1) + geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = WAVES, labels = WAVE_LABEL[as.character(WAVES)]) +
  labs(title = "Mean depression caseness over time", y = "P(CIDI case)", x = NULL) + theme_minimal()

Mothers run higher than fathers throughout; both are roughly flat (no acute postpartum peak in the observed window — Year 1 ≈ 12–15 months).

4 Stage 1 — Trajectory clustering (GBTM, flexmix)

Group-based trajectory modeling on binary caseness, mothers and fathers separately, fit on the discovery sample. We enumerate k = 1..4 (multi-start), select on BIC, and check entropy and bootstrap stability.

fit_gbtm <- function(parent){
  d <- long |> filter(parent == !!parent, split == "discovery", !is.na(md_case_lib))
  st <- stepFlexmix(cbind(md_case_lib, 1 - md_case_lib) ~ poly(wave, 2) | idnum,
                    data = d, k = 1:4, model = FLXMRglm(family = "binomial"),
                    nrep = 4, verbose = FALSE)
  best <- getModel(st, "BIC")
  # per-unit modal class
  units <- d |> mutate(cl = clusters(best)) |> group_by(idnum) |>
    summarise(cl = as.integer(names(which.max(table(cl)))), .groups = "drop")
  # label persistent = higher mean caseness
  mu <- d |> mutate(cl = clusters(best)) |> group_by(cl) |> summarise(m = mean(md_case_lib), .groups="drop")
  persist <- mu$cl[which.max(mu$m)]
  units$class <- ifelse(units$cl == persist, "persistent", "rarely")
  list(step = st, best = best, units = units, data = d, mu = mu)
}
G <- lapply(c("mother","father"), fit_gbtm); names(G) <- c("mother","father")

for (p in c("mother","father")) {
  st <- G[[p]]$step
  cat(sprintf("\n[%s] BIC by k: %s | chosen k = %d\n", p,
              paste(sprintf("%.0f", BIC(st)), collapse = " "), G[[p]]$best@k))
  print(table(G[[p]]$units$class))
}
## 
## [mother] BIC by k: 9110 8328 8339 8373 | chosen k = 2
## 
## persistent     rarely 
##        488       2340 
## 
## [father] BIC by k: 5839 5498 5522 5557 | chosen k = 2
## 
## persistent     rarely 
##        318       2127
prof <- bind_rows(lapply(c("mother","father"), function(p){
  G[[p]]$data |> left_join(G[[p]]$units[,c("idnum","class")], by = "idnum") |>
    group_by(parent, class, wave) |> summarise(dep = mean(md_case_lib), .groups = "drop")
}))
ggplot(prof, aes(wave, dep, color = class)) + geom_line(linewidth = 1) + geom_point() +
  facet_wrap(~parent) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = WAVES, labels = WAVE_LABEL[as.character(WAVES)]) +
  labs(title = "Trajectory classes (discovery): rarely- vs persistently-depressed",
       y = "P(CIDI case)", x = NULL) + theme_minimal()

A clean 2-class solution for both parents: a large rarely-depressed majority and a smaller, flat-high persistently-depressed minority. The structure is trait-like, not an infant-period spike (caveat: the acute 0–6-month window is unmeasured). Entropy ≈ 0.72 — moderate classification certainty, so we treat the classes as a discretized vulnerability continuum, not crisp categories.

5 Stage 2 — Predictors of the persistent class

Does pre-existing vulnerability (fh) or SES drive persistent-class membership? We use flexmix’s concomitant (covariate → class) model, which estimates the membership multinomial jointly with the trajectory model. Predictors are standardized (OR per SD).

stage2 <- function(parent){
  fhcol <- paste0("fh_", parent)
  d <- long |> filter(parent == !!parent, !is.na(md_case_lib)) |>
    left_join(vuln[, c("idnum", fhcol)], by = "idnum") |> rename(fh = !!fhcol) |>
    filter(!is.na(fh), !is.na(poverty_cat), !is.na(par_edu))
  d$fhz <- z(d$fh); d$povz <- z(d$poverty_cat); d$eduz <- z(d$par_edu)
  m <- flexmix(cbind(md_case_lib, 1 - md_case_lib) ~ poly(wave, 2) | idnum, data = d, k = 2,
               model = FLXMRglm(family = "binomial"),
               concomitant = FLXPmultinom(~ fhz + povz + eduz))
  mu <- sapply(1:2, function(k) weighted.mean(d$md_case_lib, posterior(m)[, k]))
  persist <- which.max(mu)
  cf <- m@concomitant@coef
  nonref <- which(colSums(abs(cf)) > 1e-8)[1]
  s <- if (persist == nonref) 1 else -1
  data.frame(parent = parent, predictor = c("family history (fh)","poverty ratio","education"),
             OR_per_SD = exp(s * cf[c("fhz","povz","eduz"), nonref]))
}
s2 <- bind_rows(lapply(c("mother","father"), stage2))
knitr::kable(s2, digits = 3, row.names = FALSE,
             caption = "Stage-2: OR per SD for persistent-class membership (concomitant model)")
Stage-2: OR per SD for persistent-class membership (concomitant model)
parent predictor OR_per_SD
mother family history (fh) 2.567
mother poverty ratio 0.767
mother education 0.855
father family history (fh) 2.411
father poverty ratio 0.873
father education 0.706

Family history strongly dominates. Its OR/SD dwarfs both SES terms, which are weak and run in the expected direction (lower SES → modestly higher persistence): persistent parental depression is mostly continuation of pre-existing vulnerability, and the SES→depression link is largely confounded by that vulnerability.

Robustness note. A bias-adjusted three-step (BCH) on a held-out subsample (run separately) gives the same qualitative conclusion with a more conservative fh OR (~1.3/SD). The one-step concomitant is more efficient but can overstate class separation; both agree that vulnerability ≫ SES.

6 Build the within-person Village panel

Exposure is taken at the prior wave (support → subsequent depression), so each row is a transition Yt₋₁ → Yt with the lagged Village facets.

# fh in long form + a wave-level `base` (used by the reverse-lag and synthesis sections)
fh_long <- vuln |> select(idnum, fh_mother, fh_father) |>
  pivot_longer(-idnum, names_to = "parent", values_to = "fh") |>
  mutate(parent = sub("fh_", "", parent))

base <- long |> select(idnum, parent, wave, md_case_lib, md_case_con, poverty_cat, par_edu) |>
  left_join(support_long, by = c("idnum","parent","wave")) |>
  left_join(fh_long, by = c("idnum","parent"))

# the lagged-transition panel (prior-wave exposure -> next-wave caseness) is consumed
# DIRECTLY from the canonical mart — Y1->Y3, Y3->Y5, Y5->Y9 construction, with the
# perceived/enacted/grandparent facets alongside v_instrumental.
panel <- read.csv(MARTS$panel) |> mutate(idnum = as.integer(idnum)) |>
  filter(!is.na(dep_t), !is.na(dep_prev))
write.csv(panel, file.path(PROC, "rmd_village_panel_tv.csv"), row.names = FALSE)
cat(sprintf("Panel: %d transition-rows, %d families\n", nrow(panel), length(unique(panel$idnum))))
## Panel: 18928 transition-rows, 4456 families
# Does support actually vary WITHIN person? (the identification base)
for (p in c("mother","father")) {
  d <- panel |> filter(parent == p, !is.na(v_instrumental)) |>
    group_by(idnum) |> mutate(pm = mean(v_instrumental)) |> ungroup()
  multi <- d |> group_by(idnum) |> filter(n() >= 2)
  cat(sprintf("%s: between-person SD %.3f | within-person SD %.3f\n",
              p, sd(d$pm), sd(d$v_instrumental - d$pm)))
}
## mother: between-person SD 0.251 | within-person SD 0.139
## father: between-person SD 0.237 | within-person SD 0.130

Within-person SD is a substantial fraction of between-person SD — support genuinely changes within individuals, so within-person effects are identifiable.

7 The causal ladder — reader’s guide

The sections below are not parallel analyses — they are a ladder climbing from the weakest causal claim to the strongest, each rung closing the loophole the rung beneath it leaves open. A finding that survives all four has had every plausible “but couldn’t it just be…?” tested and individually rejected. All four read against the same outcome (next-wave CIDI caseness), and — crucially — the Stage-1 cluster labels do not enter any rung, so the causal verdict is independent of the (fuzzy) clustering.

Rung Test Removes / answers Leaves open →
1 Within-person (Mundlak + FE logit) all time-invariant confounding — each parent is their own control time-varying confounding
2 E-value sensitivity how strong an unmeasured confounder must be to erase the surviving effect (quantifies, does not remove)
3 Cross-lagged reverse test reverse causation (depression → lower future support) —
4 Terminal new-onset subgroup tests the thesis directly in its cleanest pro-thesis slice —

8 Rung 1 — Within-person modeling (removes stable confounding)

Rung 1 of 4 · the core test. Removes: every time-invariant confounder (genes, upbringing, stable disposition) by using each parent as their own control. Leaves open: time-varying confounding — a life shock that moves support and mood together — addressed at Rung 2.

We decompose lagged support (Mundlak / within-between): person-mean = the between-person term (confounded by stable disposition); wave-deviation = the within-person “mitigation” term. Two estimators: a Mundlak-decomposed GEE logit (cluster-robust) and a conditional fixed-effects logit (pure within).

  # 95% CI from a GEE coefficient row: exp(Estimate ± 1.96*Std.err)
  ci <- function(cf, term) exp(cf[term, "Estimate"] + c(-1.96, 1.96) * cf[term, "Std.err"])

  # one village facet at a time -> Mundlak between/within decomposition (the facet ablation)
  fit_within <- function(parent, facet = "v_perceived"){
    d <- panel |> filter(parent == !!parent) |>
      select(idnum, dep_t, dep_prev, wave_out, x = all_of(facet), fh, poverty_cat, par_edu) |> na.omit()
    d$x <- z(d$x); d$poverty_cat <- z(d$poverty_cat); d$par_edu <- z(d$par_edu)
    d <- d |> group_by(idnum) |> mutate(x_ib = mean(x), x_iw = x - x_ib) |>
      ungroup() |> arrange(idnum)
    # FE-informative sample — distinguishes a PRECISE within-null from an underpowered one.
    # The clogit likelihood uses only outcome-discordant strata; the x_iw coefficient is only
    # identified for parents whose exposure ALSO moves -> report both counts alongside the OR.
    inf <- d |> group_by(idnum) |>
      summarise(out_disc = dplyr::n_distinct(dep_t) > 1,
                exp_var  = (max(x_iw) - min(x_iw)) > 1e-9, .groups = "drop")
    g <- geeglm(dep_t ~ dep_prev + factor(wave_out) + x_ib + x_iw + fh + poverty_cat + par_edu + x_iw:fh,
                id = idnum, data = d, family = binomial, corstr = "exchangeable")
    cg <- summary(g)$coefficients
    # binary facets (grandparent/religion/enacted) may have too few within-person switchers
    # for the conditional FE logit -> guard it and report NA rather than aborting the table.
    cc <- tryCatch(summary(clogit(dep_t ~ dep_prev + x_iw + I(x_iw*fh) + factor(wave_out) +
                                  strata(idnum), data = d))$coefficients,
                   error = function(e) NULL)
    bt <- ci(cg, "x_ib"); wn <- ci(cg, "x_iw")
    data.frame(
      facet            = facet, parent = parent,
      n_persons        = dplyr::n_distinct(d$idnum),
      n_out_disc       = sum(inf$out_disc),                  # outcome-discordant (clogit-informative)
      n_fe_units       = sum(inf$out_disc & inf$exp_var),    # ALSO exposure-moving (identify x_iw)
      between_OR       = exp(cg["x_ib","Estimate"]), between_lo = bt[1], between_hi = bt[2],
      between_p        = cg["x_ib","Pr(>|W|)"],
      within_GEE_OR    = exp(cg["x_iw","Estimate"]), within_lo  = wn[1], within_hi  = wn[2],
      within_GEE_p     = cg["x_iw","Pr(>|W|)"],
      within_clogit_OR = if (!is.null(cc)) exp(cc["x_iw","coef"]) else NA_real_,
      within_clogit_p  = if (!is.null(cc)) cc["x_iw","Pr(>|z|)"] else NA_real_,
      buffering_OR     = exp(cg["x_iw:fh","Estimate"]), buffering_p = cg["x_iw:fh","Pr(>|W|)"]
    )
  }
  FACETS <- c("v_perceived", "v_enacted", "v_grandparent", "v_religion")
  W <- bind_rows(lapply(FACETS, function(f)
         bind_rows(lapply(c("mother","father"), fit_within, facet = f))))
  knitr::kable(W, digits = 3, row.names = FALSE,
               caption = "Between- vs within-person effect of each village facet on next-wave depression")
Between- vs within-person effect of each village facet on next-wave depression
facet parent n_persons n_out_disc n_fe_units between_OR between_lo between_hi between_p within_GEE_OR within_lo within_hi within_GEE_p within_clogit_OR within_clogit_p buffering_OR buffering_p
v_perceived mother 4098 954 735 0.762 0.714 0.814 0.000 1.080 0.928 1.257 0.322 0.966 0.696 1.028 0.787
v_perceived father 3119 511 356 0.764 0.707 0.826 0.000 0.988 0.821 1.188 0.894 0.940 0.602 1.055 0.704
v_enacted mother 4126 1001 472 1.409 1.314 1.510 0.000 0.908 0.807 1.021 0.107 1.027 0.689 1.001 0.994
v_enacted father 2813 370 125 1.246 1.132 1.371 0.000 0.737 0.600 0.905 0.004 0.855 0.243 1.385 0.026
v_grandparent mother 4118 989 272 1.050 0.978 1.128 0.178 0.987 0.868 1.122 0.841 1.040 0.575 0.929 0.400
v_grandparent father 3081 474 155 1.049 0.951 1.157 0.336 1.019 0.889 1.167 0.790 1.159 0.163 0.896 0.335
v_religion mother 4127 1002 364 0.941 0.878 1.009 0.089 1.087 0.958 1.233 0.197 1.052 0.523 0.887 0.190
v_religion father 3140 533 166 0.917 0.835 1.007 0.071 1.114 0.917 1.353 0.275 1.082 0.461 0.990 0.943

The facet ablation. Decomposed one facet at a time, the four village axes behave very differently — which is exactly why they are kept separate rather than pooled into a single “support” index:

  • Perceived support (the headline) is strongly protective between persons (OR ≈ 0.76, both parents) but null within persons across both estimators (OR ≈ 1.0), with a null buffering interaction. The protective look is a between-person phenomenon consistent with stable confounding, not a within-person mitigation effect.
  • Enacted support runs the opposite way between persons (OR ≈ 1.4 mother / 1.2 father) — the support-mobilization signature (help is sought when distressed). For fathers there is a within-person protective signal in the GEE (OR 0.74, p = .004) with fh-buffering, but it is not confirmed by the conditional FE logit (OR 0.86, p = .24) → suggestive, not robust.
  • Grandparent co-residence is null on every axis (between OR ≈ 1.05, ns; within null).
  • Religious participation shows only a weak, non-significant between-person protective hint (OR 0.94 / 0.92, p ≈ .08) and a null within-person effect (and a null predictive contribution; see the companion notebook). It neither overturns nor adds to the story.

Like perceived support, participation is at most a weak between-person correlate with no within-person lever.

  fp <- bind_rows(
    W |> transmute(facet, parent, term = "between (v_ib)", OR = between_OR, lo = between_lo, hi = between_hi),
    W |> transmute(facet, parent, term = "within (v_iw)",  OR = within_GEE_OR, lo = within_lo, hi = within_hi)) |>
    mutate(facet = factor(facet, levels = c("v_perceived","v_enacted","v_grandparent","v_religion")))
  ggplot(fp, aes(OR, parent, color = term)) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = 1, linetype = 2, color = "grey50") +
    geom_pointrange(aes(xmin = lo, xmax = hi), position = position_dodge(0.5), linewidth = 0.8) +
    facet_wrap(~ facet, ncol = 1) +
    scale_x_log10() +
    labs(title = "Each village facet → next-wave depression: between vs within (95% CI)",
         x = "Odds ratio (log scale)", y = NULL, color = NULL) +
    theme_minimal() + theme(legend.position = "top")

9 Rung 2 — Sensitivity to unmeasured confounding (E-value)

Rung 2 of 4 · the stress test. Rung 1 cannot remove time-varying confounding, so rather than remove it we bound it: how strong would an unmeasured confounder have to be — associated with both support and depression — to fully explain the effect that survives? Applied to the headline between-person perceived-support effect (the only non-null one).

# How strong an unmeasured confounder would need to be to explain away the between-person
# effect — E-value for the point estimate AND the CI limit nearest the null (VanderWeele & Ding 2017).
base_p0 <- tapply(panel$dep_t, panel$parent, function(v) mean(v, na.rm = TRUE))  # baseline outcome prevalence by parent
W |>
  filter(facet == "v_perceived") |>             # E-value interpretable for the headline protective effect
  transmute(parent, between_OR, between_hi,
            p0       = base_p0[parent],          # named lookup → robust to row order & multi-facet
            RR_point = or2rr(between_OR, p0),  E_point = evalue(RR_point),
            RR_CI    = or2rr(between_hi, p0),  E_CI    = evalue(RR_CI)) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3,
    caption = "E-value for the between-person PERCEIVED-support effect (point estimate and CI limit nearest the null)")
E-value for the between-person PERCEIVED-support effect (point estimate and CI limit nearest the null)
parent between_OR between_hi p0 RR_point E_point RR_CI E_CI
mother 0.762 0.814 0.1841276 0.7972346 1.819157 0.8429471 1.656449
father 0.764 0.826 0.1309569 0.7887595 1.850512 0.8453249 1.648227

An unmeasured confounder associated with both perceived support and depression by a risk ratio of only ~1.8 (point estimate; ~1.6 at the CI limit nearest the null) would fully explain the between-person association — and stable negative affectivity clears that easily.

10 Rung 3 — Cross-lagged reverse test (rules out reverse causation)

Rung 3 of 4 · the direction check. Removes: the possibility the arrow runs backward — depression eroding future support rather than support protecting against depression. A within-person reverse-null, paired with the forward-null, is the fingerprint of a stable common cause (confounding), not causation in either direction.

Does depression at t predict lower support at t+1 (controlling support at t)? If the within-person reverse effect is also null, the relationship is purely a stable between-person bundle.

sup_wave <- base |> select(idnum, parent, wave, support = v_instrumental, dep = md_case_lib,
                           fh, poverty_cat, par_edu)
rev_pairs <- do.call(rbind, lapply(list(c(2,3), c(3,4), c(4,5)), function(pr){
  a <- sup_wave |> filter(wave == pr[1]); b <- sup_wave |> filter(wave == pr[2])
  b |> select(idnum, parent, support_t1 = support) |>
    inner_join(a |> select(idnum, parent, dep_t0 = dep, support_t0 = support, fh, poverty_cat, par_edu),
               by = c("idnum","parent"))
}))
rev_tab <- bind_rows(lapply(c("mother","father"), function(p){
  d <- rev_pairs |> filter(parent == p) |> na.omit() |> arrange(idnum)
  d <- d |> group_by(idnum) |> mutate(dep_btw = mean(dep_t0), dep_wth = dep_t0 - dep_btw) |> ungroup()
  m <- geeglm(support_t1 ~ dep_btw + dep_wth + support_t0 + fh + poverty_cat + par_edu,
              id = idnum, data = d, family = gaussian, corstr = "exchangeable")
  cf <- summary(m)$coefficients
  # FE cross-check: an INDEPENDENT within-OLS (person-demeaned) estimate of the reverse effect,
  # mirroring the forward test's dual-estimator design. It is not numerically identical to the
  # GEE within term (GEE uses an exchangeable working correlation, OLS does not), but both should
  # land near zero. Also report how many parents have discordant prior depression.
  dw <- d |> group_by(idnum) |>
    mutate(dplyr::across(c(support_t1, dep_t0, support_t0), ~ .x - mean(.x))) |> ungroup()
  fe <- lm(support_t1 ~ dep_t0 + support_t0 + 0, data = dw)
  n_disc <- d |> group_by(idnum) |> summarise(v = (max(dep_t0) - min(dep_t0)) > 0, .groups = "drop")
  data.frame(parent = p, n_dep_discordant = sum(n_disc$v),
             between_dep_effect = cf["dep_btw","Estimate"], between_p = cf["dep_btw","Pr(>|W|)"],
             within_dep_effect  = cf["dep_wth","Estimate"], within_p  = cf["dep_wth","Pr(>|W|)"],
             within_FE_lm       = unname(coef(fe)["dep_t0"]))
}))
knitr::kable(rev_tab, digits = 4, row.names = FALSE,
             caption = "Reverse lag: depression -> change in next-wave support")
Reverse lag: depression -> change in next-wave support
parent n_dep_discordant between_dep_effect between_p within_dep_effect within_p within_FE_lm
mother 906 -0.0507 0 0.0106 0.3440 -0.0138
father 441 -0.0463 0 0.0146 0.3482 -0.0029

The reverse arrow is also between-person only (chronically-depressed people report declining support) and null within-person. Combined with the forward null, neither arrow operates within individuals — the signature of a stable common cause, not causation in either direction.

11 Rung 4 — Terminal test: the original thesis, cleanest slice

Rung 4 of 4 · the decisive case. Tests: among low-vulnerability (fh = 0), previously-well parents — the slice where “a Village mends them” is most likely to hold — does support prevent new-onset depression within persons? If it fails even here, it fails everywhere.

“Parenting broke them, did a Village mend them?” Among low-vulnerability (fh=0), previously-well parents — the cleanest pro-thesis slice — does support prevent new-onset depression?

sub_tab <- bind_rows(lapply(c("mother","father"), function(p){
  d <- panel |> filter(parent == p, fh == 0, dep_prev == 0) |>
    select(idnum, dep_t, wave_out, v_instrumental, poverty_cat, par_edu) |> na.omit()
  d$v_instrumental <- z(d$v_instrumental); d$poverty_cat <- z(d$poverty_cat); d$par_edu <- z(d$par_edu)
  d <- d |> group_by(idnum) |> mutate(v_ib = mean(v_instrumental), v_iw = v_instrumental - v_ib) |>
    ungroup() |> arrange(idnum)
  mA <- geeglm(dep_t ~ v_instrumental + poverty_cat + par_edu + factor(wave_out),
               id = idnum, data = d, family = binomial, corstr = "exchangeable")
  mB <- geeglm(dep_t ~ v_ib + v_iw + poverty_cat + par_edu + factor(wave_out),
               id = idnum, data = d, family = binomial, corstr = "exchangeable")
  ca <- summary(mA)$coefficients; cb <- summary(mB)$coefficients
  data.frame(parent = p, new_onset_events = sum(d$dep_t),
             naive_OR = exp(ca["v_instrumental","Estimate"]),
             between_OR = exp(cb["v_ib","Estimate"]),
             within_OR = exp(cb["v_iw","Estimate"]), within_p = cb["v_iw","Pr(>|W|)"])
}))
knitr::kable(sub_tab, digits = 3, row.names = FALSE,
             caption = "Low-fh new-onset subgroup: support -> new-onset depression")
Low-fh new-onset subgroup: support -> new-onset depression
parent new_onset_events naive_OR between_OR within_OR within_p
mother 534 0.823 0.767 1.024 0.767
father 313 0.825 0.774 0.988 0.895

Same pattern even here: the naive/between association looks protective, the within-person effect is null. The Village did not mend them — supported fresh parents fare better only because they are different people.

12 Synthesis — the vulnerability triad

Ladder complete — Rungs 1–4 converge on a within-person null. This section steps off the ladder to integrate the descriptive layer (Stage-1 clustering) with the causal layer (the ladder): the answer to the title’s “cause or signature?” question.

Do the trajectory classes carry the support and family-history signatures?

syn <- bind_rows(lapply(c("mother","father"), function(p){
  pm <- support_long |> filter(parent == p) |> group_by(idnum) |>
    summarise(support_mean = mean(v_instrumental, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")
  G[[p]]$units |> left_join(pm, by = "idnum") |>
    left_join(vuln |> select(idnum, fh = !!paste0("fh_", p)), by = "idnum") |>
    group_by(class) |>
    summarise(n = n(), support_mean = mean(support_mean, na.rm = TRUE),
              fh_prev = mean(fh, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |> mutate(parent = p)
}))
knitr::kable(syn, digits = 3, caption = "Support and family-history by trajectory class")
Support and family-history by trajectory class
class n support_mean fh_prev parent
persistent 488 0.60 0.620 mother
rarely 2340 0.75 0.283 mother
persistent 318 0.67 0.594 father
rarely 2127 0.79 0.266 father

The persistent class reports markedly lower support and carries roughly double the family-history prevalence. The “vulnerable” trajectory is best read as a single stable disposition expressed as a triad — persistent depression, low perceived support, elevated family history — none of which causally moves the others within a person. Support and fh are signatures of membership, not separate causal levers.

13 Anxiety (GAD) — secondary outcome

The CIDI also yields generalized-anxiety caseness, but it was fielded only at Years 1 & 3. Two waves means no trajectory clustering and no within-person identification (a single Y1→Y3 transition cannot separate within- from between-person variation). Anxiety therefore enters as a secondary, between-person check: prevalence, comorbidity with depression, and a single prospective Y1→Y3 association.

anx <- long |> filter(wave %in% GAD_WAVES, !is.na(gad_case))
anx |> group_by(parent, wave) |>
  summarise(n = n(), gad_prev = mean(gad_case),
            phi_with_depression = cor(gad_case, md_case_lib, use = "complete.obs"), .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(child_age = WAVE_LABEL[as.character(wave)]) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3, caption = "GAD caseness prevalence and comorbidity with depression (same wave)")
GAD caseness prevalence and comorbidity with depression (same wave)
parent wave n gad_prev phi_with_depression child_age
father 2 3373 0.025 0.323 Year 1
father 3 3285 0.033 0.308 Year 3
mother 2 4357 0.031 0.297 Year 1
mother 3 4221 0.045 0.325 Year 3

GAD is much rarer than depression (~2.5–5%) and heavily comorbid — most anxiety cases are also depression cases (φ ≈ 0.3), so anxiety is largely a severity marker within the same distress dimension, not an independent outcome.

gad_w <- long |> filter(wave %in% GAD_WAVES) |> select(idnum, parent, wave, gad_case) |>
  tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = wave, values_from = gad_case, names_prefix = "gad_w")
sup1 <- support_long |> filter(wave == 2) |> select(idnum, parent, support_y1 = v_instrumental)
cov1 <- long |> filter(wave == 2) |> select(idnum, parent, poverty_cat, par_edu) |> distinct()
anx_tab <- bind_rows(lapply(c("mother","father"), function(p){
  d <- gad_w |> left_join(sup1, by = c("idnum","parent")) |>
    left_join(fh_long, by = c("idnum","parent")) |> left_join(cov1, by = c("idnum","parent")) |>
    filter(parent == p) |>
    select(gad_w3, support_y1, gad_w2, fh, poverty_cat, par_edu) |> na.omit()
  d$support_y1 <- z(d$support_y1); d$poverty_cat <- z(d$poverty_cat); d$par_edu <- z(d$par_edu)
  m <- glm(gad_w3 ~ support_y1 + gad_w2 + fh + poverty_cat + par_edu, data = d, family = binomial)
  s <- summary(m)$coefficients
  data.frame(parent = p, n = nrow(d), gad_y3_events = sum(d$gad_w3),
             support_OR = exp(s["support_y1","Estimate"]), support_p = s["support_y1","Pr(>|z|)"],
             fh_OR = exp(s["fh","Estimate"]), fh_p = s["fh","Pr(>|z|)"])
}))
knitr::kable(anx_tab, digits = 3, row.names = FALSE,
             caption = "Y1 support -> Y3 anxiety (between-person; controlling Y1 anxiety, fh, SES)")
Y1 support -> Y3 anxiety (between-person; controlling Y1 anxiety, fh, SES)
parent n gad_y3_events support_OR support_p fh_OR fh_p
mother 3669 162 0.807 0.007 3.975 0
father 2626 73 0.688 0.000 4.264 0

This is a between-person prospective association only (the within-person test is not identifiable with two waves) and is low-powered (few GAD events). Read it as a directional check on whether the depression pattern extends to anxiety — not as a co-equal result.

14 Conclusions & limitations

Findings.

  1. Parental depression is trait-like / vulnerability-driven, not an infant-period spike; clean 2-class structure for both parents.
  2. Family-history vulnerability dominates SES in predicting the persistent class; SES is largely confounded by vulnerability.
  3. Social support (“the Village”) is protective between persons but null within persons (both estimators, both parents), with a null buffering interaction. This holds across a four-facet ablation: perceived support (protective-between/null-within), enacted support (mobilization, reverse-signed between; a non-robust father within-effect), grandparent co-residence (null throughout), and religious participation (a weak, non-significant between-person correlate with no within-person effect and no added predictive signal).
  4. The cross-lagged test is symmetric: no within-person dynamics in either direction → a stable between-person bundle (confounding), not causation.
  5. The low-vulnerability new-onset terminal test confirms it: the Village did not prevent onset within individuals.
  6. Synthesis: the vulnerable trajectory class is one disposition surfacing as a triad (persistent depression + low support + family history).

Limitations.

  • Within-person designs remove time-invariant confounding only; residual time-varying confounding cannot be excluded — so this is “no within-person effect after removing stable confounding,” not a proven zero causal effect.
  • Analytic scope & precision of the within-person tests. §4–§6 are pre-specified within-person tests with no class enumeration or model selection, so they are fit on the full sample (not the discovery split used for Stage-1 GBTM enumeration) — pooling maximizes power with no selective-inference cost here. They are complete-case (na.omit); facet coverage is high (≥90% for v_instrumental per the mart contract), so <10% of transition-rows drop, under an unverifiable MAR assumption. The conditional-FE estimates identify off outcome-and-exposure-discordant parents only — reported as n_fe_units (≈735 mothers / ≈356 fathers for perceived support) — so the within-nulls are reasonably-powered nulls, not empty cells; the binary structural facets (grandparent, religion) have far fewer switchers and their within-nulls are correspondingly weaker.
  • fh is a single retrospective family-history report (Year 3, Year-5 fill-in for coverage) → mood-congruent recall bias cannot be mitigated (the Year-5 items have zero overlap with Year 3, so no test–retest is possible); entropy ≈ 0.72 means the trajectory classes are fuzzy (treat as a continuum).
  • Binary CIDI caseness detects only threshold crossings (no within-case severity gradient); no repeated continuous CES-D exists for both parents.
  • Baseline depression measurement is asymmetric. Only fathers received a baseline depression battery — a 12-of-20-item CES-D subset with a “days in the past week” metric for which FFCWS provides no established scoring (used descriptively above, not in any model here). Mothers received no baseline depression measure (deferred to the Year-1 CIDI), so a clean pre-outcome own-depression anchor exists for fathers only; both parents’ models here rely on family history fh as the shared pre-existing-vulnerability proxy.
  • Anxiety (GAD) was fielded only at Years 1 & 3 → it supports only a between-person Y1→Y3 check (no trajectory or within-person analysis), is rare (~2.5–5%), and is heavily comorbid with depression.
  • Unweighted (FFCWS oversamples disadvantaged births) — associations, not population point estimates.
sessionInfo()
